metadata.content:
--- Page 1 ---
SECTION IV: JUDGMENT  
Issues for Consideration

Whether the accused entered into a criminal conspiracy to misappropriate public funds?

Whether the accused abused a position of dominance amounting to criminal misconduct?

Whether forged documents were used to induce wrongful release of funds?
Analysis and Findings

The court notes that conspiracy is seldom proved by direct evidence and must be inferred from 
conduct and surrounding circumstances. The consistent pattern of inflated invoices, premature fund 
release, and routing of funds establishes a concerted scheme.

The defense argument that the accused was merely a consultant is contradicted by email trails and 
witness testimony establishing de facto control over decisions.

The forensic audit and expert testimony conclusively establish fabrication and backdating of 
documents. The objection regarding electronic evidence is rejected as proper certification under 
Section 65B of the Evidence Act has been produced.

Conclusion

The prosecution has proved beyond reasonable doubt that:

The accused dishonestly induced the government to release funds

Public servants were influenced to act illegally

Forged documents were knowingly used

Final Order  
--- Page 2 ---

The accused Arjun Malhotra is convicted under:

Sections 120B, 420, 468, 471 IPC

Section 13(2) read with Section 13(1)(d) of the Prevention of Corruption Act

Sentence to be heard separately.

--- Page 1 ---
SECTION I: CHARGE SHEET (u/s 173 CrPC)
IN THE COURT OF THE HON’BLE SESSIONS COURT AT BENGALURU

Case Title: State of Karnataka vs. Arjun Malhotra  
FIR No.: 342/2020  
Police Station: Whitefield Police Station  
Sections Invoked:  
Sections 120B, 406, 409, 420, 468, 471 IPC  
Section 13(1)(d) read with Section 13(2) Prevention of Corruption Act, 1988

Date of Filing of Charge Sheet: 22 February 2021

Prosecution Case in Brief

The present case arises out of a large -scale financial fraud involving public funds sanctioned for the 
construction of affordable housing units under the “Urban Shelter Development Scheme (USDS)” 
during the financial years 2017 –2019.

The accused, Arjun Malhotra, was functioning as the Project Consultant and de facto decision -maker 
for the execution of housing projects in coordination with the Bruhat Bengaluru Mahanagara Palike 
(BBMP). It is alleged that the accused entered into a crimi nal conspiracy with unknown public 
servants and private contractors to misappropriate government funds.

Between April 2017 and December 2019, the accused allegedly:

Inflated project costs

Submitted forged utilization certificates

Approved payments for non -existent construction work

Diverted funds through shell companies  
--- Page 2 ---

Detailed Allegations

The investigation revealed that:

A total sum of ₹18.75 crores was released in phases by the Urban Development Department.

Physical inspection revealed that only 42% of construction work was completed.

Despite this, 100% utilization certificates were submitted.

Fake invoices and forged work completion reports were generated.

The accused allegedly exercised control over fund disbursement despite lacking statutory authority, 
thereby abusing his dominant position to influence public servants.
Documentary and Electronic Evidence
Sanction orders issued by Urban Development Department

Forged utilization certificates

Bank statements of shell companies

Email correspondence showing coordination with contractors

Forensic audit report

Site inspection reports

Digital signatures used on forged documents  
--- Page 3 ---

List of Accused

Accused No. 1: Arjun Malhotra (Project Consultant)

Accused No. 2 –5: Unknown public servants (under investigation)

PW-5 stated that digital signatures affixed on utilization certificates were cloned and misused. 
Metadata analysis indicated backdated document creation.

--- Page 1 ---
SECTION II: WITNESS STATEMENTS (u/s 161 CrPC)  
Statement of PW -1 – Government Auditor

PW-1 stated that during routine audit, discrepancies were found between physical construction 
progress and funds released. PW -1 observed repeated submission of identical photographs across 
different utilization certificates, indicating fabrication.

PW-1 further stated that objections raised were ignored due to “administrative pressure.”

Statement of PW -2 – Junior Engineer (BBMP)

PW-2 stated that he was instructed orally by senior officials to sign completion reports without 
conducting site inspections. PW -2 identified the accused as the person coordinating communications 
between contractors and municipal officials.

Statement of PW -3 – Contractor

PW-3 admitted that his firm was awarded contracts despite lacking eligibility. PW -3 stated that 
invoices were prepared based on instructions from the accused and that a fixed percentage of funds 
was routed back through intermediary accounts.

Statement of PW -4 – Bank Manager

PW-4 produced account statements showing rapid fund transfers from contractor accounts to shell 
companies controlled by the accused’s relatives. PW -4 confirmed that no legitimate business activity 
was associated with these entities.

Statement of PW -5 – Forensic Expert

--- Page 2 ---
SECTION III: HEARING NOTES / ORDER SHEETS  
Order dated 14 March 2021

Accused produced on summons. Application for anticipatory bail rejected considering economic 
offence involving public funds.
Order dated 30 June 2021

Charge framed under Sections 120B, 409, 420, 468, 471 IPC and Prevention of Corruption Act. 
Accused pleaded not guilty.
Order dated 19 October 2021

Examination of PW -1 and PW -2 completed. Defense raised objection regarding admissibility of 
electronic evidence. Objection kept open.
Order dated 07 February 2022
Forensic audit report marked as Exhibit P -17. Cross -examination of PW -5 deferred.
Order dated 11 August 2022

Defense application seeking discharge rejected. Court observed prima facie evidence of conspiracy 
and abuse of position.

Order dated 25 January 2023

Final arguments concluded. Matter reserved for judgment.
